text: Clean Architecture by Robert C. Martin defines architecture as the shape given to a system that minimizes the human effort required to build and maintain it. Good architecture maximizes the number of decisions not yet made — it keeps options open as long as possible rather than committing to frameworks, databases, and infrastructure early.

Clean Architecture — The Dependency Rule: source code dependencies must point only inward. Inner layers know nothing about outer layers. Business rules do not know about databases, UI, or frameworks. This means the database is a detail, the web framework is a detail, the UI is a detail — all replaceable without touching business logic.

Clean Architecture — Layers: Entities contain enterprise-wide business rules. Use Cases contain application-specific business rules. Interface Adapters convert data between use cases and external formats. Frameworks and Drivers are the outermost layer — databases, web frameworks, devices. The further out, the more likely to change. Protect inner layers from outer layer volatility.

Clean Architecture — Entities vs Use Cases: Entities are business objects with rules that would exist even without software — a loan has interest, an order has a total. Use Cases are application-specific flows — place an order, approve a loan. Use Cases orchestrate Entities. A change in a Use Case should not affect Entities. A change in an Entity may affect Use Cases but never the opposite.

Clean Architecture — The humble object pattern separates testable logic from hard-to-test behavior. UI presenters, database gateways, and service adapters are humble objects — thin, mostly untestable wrappers. The logic they coordinate is extracted into testable objects. This pattern makes the architecture testable at every layer without needing the full stack running.

Clean Architecture — common misconception: it does not mean four specific layers always. It means the Dependency Rule applies consistently. Some systems need more layers, some need fewer. The rule is direction of dependency, not number of layers. Forcing a project into four layers when two are sufficient adds unnecessary complexity.
